behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in their own category and are not also counted as a deletion plus an addition.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure can manifest at two boundaries — git's rename detection (which uses a similarity index and only fires under real git conditions) and the tool's parsing of git status codes (e.g., 'R' vs 'D'+'A'). Testing only the parser with a fixture string would miss the first failure mode entirely. REAL-INFRA: Git rename detection is a real subprocess behavior that depends on git's internal similarity threshold and diff algorithm. An in-process fake cannot reproduce the actual rename-detection path: git may report a rename as two separate events if the threshold isn't met, and only a real git subprocess will expose that. REJECTION-GATE: Integration (mocked subprocess) was considered but rejected because the behavior explicitly concerns git's categorization of renames, not just the tool's string parser — a stubbed subprocess output would bake in whatever format the stub author assumed, hiding any mismatch between expected and actual git output.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temporary git repository, add a file, commit it, then rename it using `git mv` and commit again. Run the CLI tool against this repo and assert: (1) exactly one renamed entry appears in the 'renamed' category with the correct old→new path pair, (2) the 'deleted' count does not include the renamed file, (3) the 'added' count does not include the renamed file.

behaviors.description: Diffs that include binary files do not cause the tool to crash; binary files are counted in the output.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The crash would come from the tool attempting to decode or process bytes it receives from a real git diff of a binary file. Git emits a specific 'Binary files differ' marker in its diff output for binary content, and the tool must handle this gracefully. REAL-INFRA: The exact output format git produces for binary files (including null bytes in raw diffs, the 'Binary files ... differ' line in text diffs, and the binary file count in `--stat`) is real subprocess behavior. An in-process stub could reproduce the surface string, but whether the tool correctly handles the byte stream without crashing requires the real git subprocess path to be exercised. MOCK-HIDE: A stub that returns a clean 'Binary files differ' string would always pass, even if the tool crashes on the actual bytes git emits. REJECTION-GATE: Integration was considered but rejected because the failure mode is a crash triggered by real git binary-diff output format, not by module wiring logic.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temporary git repository, commit a binary file (e.g., a small PNG or a file written with `\x00` bytes), then modify it and stage/commit the change. Run the CLI tool and assert: (1) the process exits with code 0 (no crash), (2) the binary file appears in the 'modified' category with a count of 1, (3) no exception stack trace appears in stdout or stderr.

behaviors.description: Running the tool on a repository that has no commits yet produces an empty report rather than an error.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA: Git's behavior on a repo with no commits (unborn HEAD) is a real subprocess edge case. Commands like `git log`, `git diff`, and `git status` all produce distinct exit codes and output (or errors) for an unborn HEAD that differ from a repo with history. Only a real git subprocess will reproduce this exact error path. MOCK-HIDE: A subprocess stub returning an empty string would not capture the actual non-zero exit code or error message git emits (e.g., 'fatal: your current branch … does not have any commits yet'), so a stubbed test would always pass even if the tool crashes on the real error. REJECTION-GATE: Unit and Integration were considered but both require knowledge of the exact error string git emits for an unborn HEAD — that knowledge can only be validated against a real git process.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temporary directory and run `git init` in it (but make no commits). Run the CLI tool targeting this empty repository and assert: (1) the process exits with code 0, (2) stdout is valid structured output (e.g., parseable JSON or the expected report format), (3) all change categories (added, deleted, renamed, modified) report a count of zero or are absent, (4) no error message or stack trace appears.

behaviors.description: The counts in the report match the actual change history of the repository being analyzed.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Count accuracy is an end-to-end property: the tool must correctly invoke git, parse its output, deduplicate entries (e.g., a rename must not inflate added+deleted), and aggregate counts. Any of these steps can introduce an off-by-one or double-counting error that only shows up when the full pipeline runs against real history. REAL-INFRA: To assert that counts match 'the actual change history', the test must create a real git repo with a known, deterministic set of operations (N adds, M deletes, K renames, J modifications) and then verify the tool's output against those exact numbers. This requires a real git subprocess. MOCK-HIDE: A stubbed git output would be crafted by the test author with the same assumptions as the tool's parser — any shared blind spot would cause both to agree while the real tool fails on real repos. REJECTION-GATE: Integration was considered but can only test that the tool correctly parses a fixed string, not that the string it reads from a real git process maps to the correct counts.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temporary git repository and execute a precisely controlled sequence: add 3 files and commit, delete 1 file and commit, rename 1 file and commit, modify 2 files and commit. Run the CLI tool and assert the report shows exactly: added=3, deleted=1, renamed=1, modified=2 (and that the renamed file is NOT double-counted in deleted or added). Also verify total file count equals 5 distinct paths.

behaviors.description: The output is identical whether the changes being analyzed are staged or unstaged.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA: The distinction between staged and unstaged is a real git index concept. Git uses different diff commands for each (`git diff --cached` vs `git diff`) and they may produce different output for the same logical change. Only a real git subprocess with a real index state can reproduce the exact divergence. MOCK-HIDE: A stub that returns the same string for both staged and unstaged queries would always pass, completely hiding any inconsistency in which git command the tool invokes. REJECTION-GATE: Integration was considered but rejected because the failure mode is specifically about whether the tool invokes the correct git command variants for each state — this requires a real git index that can be in both staged and unstaged states independently.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temporary git repository with an initial commit. Then make the same set of changes (add 2 files, modify 1, delete 1) twice: once leave them unstaged (working tree only), run the tool and capture output A; then `git add -A` to stage all changes, run the tool again and capture output B. Assert output A equals output B in all category counts and file paths.
